squash.notes: Resistant to squash vine borer. Cures and stores 3-6 months.
Eat within 2 months of harvest; flavor declines in long storage.
Long vines need 6+ feet of space. Flesh strands when raked with fork after roasting.
Largest of the storage squashes (10-20 lbs). Bumpy blue-green skin stores all winter.
Long fruits 18-24 inches; cure and store for winter. Pink Banana is the heirloom standard.
Ornamental and edible; sweet flesh. Distinctive cap-shape; popular for fall display.

squash.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Italian Heirloom Squash
squash.notes: Italian zucchini heirloom; ribbed cylindrical fruit, denser sweeter flesh than common zucchini. Pick at 6-8 inches. Male flowers exceptional for stuffing.

squash.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Italian Heirloom Squash
squash.notes: Venetian heirloom; warty blue-green skin, sweet dense orange flesh. Long-keeping winter squash. Traditional pumpkin for ravioli.

squash.source: OSU Extension Service - Heirloom Winter Squash
squash.notes: Australian heirloom; ribbed slate-blue fruit, dry sweet flesh. Stores 6+ months in cool dry conditions.

squash.source: OSU Extension Service - Heirloom Winter Squash
squash.notes: Australian-New Zealand heirloom; flattened blue-grey 6-10 lb pumpkin. Lower-water flesh, excellent for pies and roasting.

squash.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - French Heirloom Squash
squash.notes: French heirloom from Eysines (Bordeaux); peanut-warty pink-salmon skin, sweet smooth flesh. The warts develop from natural sugars exuding through skin.
Japanese teardrop-shaped winter squash; sweet chestnut flavor, edible thin skin. 4-7 lb fruit. Vining habit.
Single-serving (1 lb) ivory-and-green striped winter squash; sweet flesh, edible skin. Bake whole and stuff.
Hubbard-type Sioux heirloom; teardrop-shaped, orange-red mottled skin. Sweet dense flesh; revived from indigenous seed banks.

squash.source: Cornell University vegetable breeding; Cornell Cooperative Extension
squash.notes: Cornell-revived heirloom Cucurbita pepo winter squash; small cylindrical cream-and-green-striped fruits, thin edible skin, sweet flesh. Bush-type, productive in short seasons.

squash.source: OSU Extension cucurbit variety trials; Kitazawa Seed Company
squash.notes: Japanese Cucurbita maxima; squat round green-skinned fruits with dense sweet golden flesh. Sun, Cha-cha, Sweet Mama are standard cultivars. Excellent keeper.

squash.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; UMaine Extension cucurbit notes
squash.notes: 1850s New England heirloom; huge 10 to 30 lb teardrop-shaped warty squash. Dense yellow-orange flesh, the original Thanksgiving pie squash. Stores 6 months plus.

squash.source: North Dakota State University Extension; Cornell variety notes
squash.notes: 1932 ND Agricultural College release; turban-shaped dark green squash with distinctive button. Dense, sweet, dry flesh, excellent for pie. 4 to 5 lb.

squash.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; UF/IFAS cucurbit notes
squash.notes: Cucurbita pepo with stringy flesh that pulls apart in spaghetti-like strands when cooked. Yellow oblong 3 to 5 lb fruits. Vegetable Spaghetti, Tivoli are standard cultivars.

squash.source: UMass Extension Vegetable Program (cucumber beetle trap crop research); Cornell Cooperative Extension
squash.notes: Slate-blue-skinned Hubbard selection; also used as a trap crop for cucumber beetle on commercial farms. 10 to 20 lb.

squash.source: Native Seeds/SEARCH; USDA-ARS Cucurbita argyrosperma collection
squash.notes: Cucurbita argyrosperma from the American Southwest / Mexico; long-necked striped squash, vine borer resistant, heat tolerant. Green-striped and white-striped strains. The traditional Three Sisters squash south of Pueblo country.

squash.source: Seed Savers Exchange; USDA-ARS heritage cultivar collection
squash.notes: 1893 American heirloom; long pink, blue, or jumbo white banana-shaped Cucurbita maxima fruits to 20 to 30 lb. Sweet dry flesh.
Distinctive multi-color turban-shaped fruits; Turk's Turban, French Turban strains. Ornamental and edible. Stores well.

squash.source: Cornell vegetable variety database; Johnny's Selected Seeds notes
squash.notes: Single-serving 4 inch round Cucurbita pepo; cream with green stripes, sweet golden flesh. Bush habit, productive in short seasons.

squash.source: Dream of Wild Health seed sovereignty project; Seed Savers Exchange
squash.notes: Plains Arikara people upper Missouri river heritage Cucurbita pepo; yellow-orange flattened pumpkin, sweet drying flesh. Repatriated through Dream of Wild Health and Seed Savers Exchange.
French heirloom round zucchini, baseball-sized. Ideal for stuffing.
Pale green Middle Eastern summer squash. Mild, used for stuffing in Lebanese cuisine.
Classic American summer squash, curved neck and bumpy yellow skin. Harvest small for best flavor.
Smooth yellow straight summer squash. Heavy bush yields, easy to harvest.
Scalloped disk-shaped summer squash. Pick young at 2 to 4 inches for tender skin.
Bright yellow patty pan, 1985 AAS winner. Easy to spot among foliage.
Mexican vining summer squash, round pale green fruit. Vine-borer resistant, heat-tolerant.
Italian climbing summer squash, long curved trumpet-shaped fruit. Vine-borer resistant, pick young.
Middle Eastern pale-green Kousa-type bush squash. Nutty flavor when picked at 4 inches.
French heirloom pumpkin, salmon-pink with warty peanut-like bumps. Sweet smooth flesh for soup.
Italian sea-green warty turban squash from Chioggia. Sweet flesh, traditional for gnocchi.
Tan flattened wheel-shaped pumpkin. Sweet butternut-related flesh, classic for pie.
French heirloom, deeply ribbed flattened pumpkin. Long-storing, sweet orange flesh.
Dark green warted hubbard, 10 to 15 lb. Long-storing, sweet orange flesh.
Classic dark green turban-topped buttercup. Sweet dry orange flesh.

squash.source: Territorial Seed Company descriptor; Seed Savers Exchange yearbook
squash.notes: Slate-gray Oregon heirloom, 10 to 15 lb. Exceptionally sweet orange flesh, stores into spring.
Native American heirloom, red-orange with green stripes. Pear-shaped, sweet flesh.
Pre-1840 banana-shaped slate-blue heirloom. Sweet dry orange flesh, exceptional keeper.
Pre-1831 orange ovate giant heirloom. Bright sweet flesh, historic American pie squash.
Classic American jack-o-lantern pumpkin, pre-1700 heirloom. Reliable for carving and pies.
Standard American Halloween carving pumpkin. Strong handle, uniform deep orange.
French heirloom, flattened deep-orange ribbed pumpkin. Sweet flesh, classic shape.
Australian heirloom, slate-blue ribbed pumpkin. Long-storing, sweet orange flesh.
Australian slate-blue deeply ribbed squash. Dense sweet flesh, excellent keeper.
Australian three-lobed gray-blue heirloom. Stores extremely well, sweet flesh.
Persian heirloom, large green and orange mottled fruit. Rich sweet flesh.
Huge butternut-relative, tan dumbbell-shaped. Exceptionally sweet, eat raw like melon.
Pre-1900 pink-skinned banana squash, can reach 40 lb. Sweet orange flesh.
Japanese heirloom, black-green warty skin cures to tan. Hazelnut-flavored flesh.
Teardrop-shaped Japanese kabocha relative, red-orange skin. Sweet chestnut-flavored flesh.
Sweet refined delicata bred by Molly Jahn at Cornell. Edible skin, exceptional flavor.
Sweet improved delicata, round loaf shape. Edible skin, dessert-quality flesh.
Compact bush form of delicata, AAS winner. Saves space, productive in small gardens.
Multi-colored acorn with green, orange, and cream blotches. Sweet flesh, ornamental.
Compact-bush mini-acorn, AAS winner. Single-serving fruit on space-saving plants.
Pre-1913 dark-green ribbed acorn squash. Classic acorn for stuffing and baking.
Small 6 to 8 lb classic pie pumpkin. Sweet smooth flesh.
Pre-1893 tan-netted pie pumpkin. Considered one of the best for pie filling.
Small 4 to 5 lb sugar pumpkin, traditional New England pie variety.
World-record giant pumpkin variety. With pampering, can exceed 1000 lb.
Reliable 50 to 100 lb pumpkin, easier than Atlantic Giant. Pink-orange skin.
Miniature 3-inch orange pumpkin. Ornamental and edible, prolific.
Miniature white pumpkin, palm-sized. Ornamental, edible while young.
AAS winner mini pumpkin, fits in palm. Compact bush plants for small spaces.
Deeply lobed tan pumpkin from southern France. Long-storing, sweet flesh for pies and roasting.
Crookneck cushaw, green stripes on cream. Vine-borer resistant southern heirloom.
Solid white crookneck cushaw. Vine-borer resistant, used for pie and roasting.
1970 AAS winner butternut. Smooth tan skin, deep orange flesh, vine-borer resistant.
AAS early butternut hybrid. Compact vines, good for short seasons.
Larger long delicata-type squash. Sweet flesh, edible skin, generous portions.
Small bush kabocha-type winter squash. Single-serving fruit, sweet orange flesh.
Small 2 to 3 lb pie pumpkin. Sweet smooth flesh, well-sized for single pies.
French heirloom flattened deep-orange ribbed pumpkin. Also known as Cinderella.
Heirloom orange-red ribbed pumpkin. Sweet flesh, regional New England variety.
Italian climbing summer squash. Pick young as zucchini or mature as winter squash.
Thai pumpkin, green warty rind. Vine-borer resistant, sweet orange flesh.
Florida Seminole heritage pumpkin. Disease- and heat-tolerant, ideal for humid southeastern gardens.
Italian round flat-topped winter squash. Sweet flesh for tortelli filling.
Italian green warted turban squash. Heritage variety from coastal Veneto.
1860s Japanese flat black-green squash. Sweet orange flesh, vine-borer resistant.
Small striped white-and-green dumpling-sized squash. Sweet for stuffing.
Golden-yellow acorn squash. Sweet flesh, beautiful color.
Japanese kabocha-maxima hybrid. Vigorous, disease-resistant, sweet dry flesh.
Classic spaghetti squash heirloom. Flesh separates into noodle-like strands when baked.
Greek heirloom flattened ribbed sweet pumpkin. Used for kolokithopita filling.
Hulless seed pumpkin. Grow for pepitas, dark green seeds need no shelling.
Austrian Styrian pumpkin grown for hulless seeds. Source of Kurbiskernol pumpkin seed oil.

alliums.notes: Closer to leek than true garlic. Mild flavor, large cloves.
Top-set bulbils flop and root, walking the patch over years. Use bulbils as scallions or replants.
Babington leek; produces top-set bulbils like walking onion. Eat tender shoots in spring.
Italian flat onion; small (1.5-3 in), sweet, ideal for roasting whole. Stores 4-6 months.

alliums.source: WSU Extension - Walla Walla Onion Production
alliums.notes: Sweet PNW heirloom; very low pungency. Poor storage - eat or process within 4-6 weeks. Long-day type.
Hardneck; rich complex flavor, easy to peel. Produces scapes in late spring (snap off for bigger bulbs). Stores 4-6 months.
Porcelain hardneck; large 4-6 cloves per bulb, white skin. Cold-hardy; one of the best PNW cultivars. Stores 6-9 months.
Hardneck; deep purple striping on white skin, reportedly the best for roasting. Chesnok Red is reliable.
Scottish 1887 heirloom; massive (1-2 lb) sweet globe onion. Long-day type for northern latitudes. Poor storage - eat or process within 1-2 months.
Louisiana heirloom; pungent purple-red short-day onion. Excellent storage, distinct flavor. For zone 6 and warmer; northerners use a long-day red instead.
Italian heirloom; large globe yellow-skinned onion, intense storage flavor. Long-day type. Stores 6+ months.
Multiplier onion - one bulb planted divides into 6-12. Save largest for replanting. Hamburger or Yellow Multiplier are common cultivars.
Early summer leek; long white shanks, slim form. Best for fresh use; not winter-hardy unlike Bandit/Bleu de Solaize.
French heirloom winter leek; blue-green leaves, very cold-hardy (-15F under mulch). Harvest through winter.
Winter-hardy leek; pale yellow-green flag leaves. Stands through winter under mulch in zone 5+.

alliums.source: Vidalia Onion Committee growing standards; University of Georgia Cooperative Extension
alliums.notes: Short-day Granex-type onion grown in 20-county Vidalia GA area for low-sulfur soil sweetness. Geographic indication protected; growers outside the AOC use other Granex strains.

alliums.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Johnny's Selected Seeds long-day onion trials
alliums.notes: Long-day yellow storage cultivars: Patterson, Cortland, Copra, Yellow Globe. Stiff papery skin, 6+ month storage. The Northeast staple.

alliums.source: UF/IFAS short- and intermediate-day onion trials; Cornell variety database
alliums.notes: Intermediate-day deep-burgundy onion; mild sweet for fresh use, modest storage life. Good crossover variety for the central US.

alliums.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension perennial allium notes; USDA-ARS Allium collection
alliums.notes: Perennial Allium x proliferum; produces topset bulbils at the top of flowering stalks. The stalk falls over, the bulbils root, and the patch walks across the bed. Cut greens like scallions year-round.

alliums.source: Texas A&M AgriLife Extension; UF/IFAS short-day onion notes
alliums.notes: Short-day flat sweet white or yellow onion; old Texas-Florida market type. Crystal Wax, White Bermuda strains.

alliums.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds long-day onion trials; Cornell variety database
alliums.notes: Long-day red storage onion; deep-red rings throughout, 4 to 6 month storage. Northeast / Midwest favorite.

alliums.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; USDA-ARS Allium collection
alliums.notes: Classic round yellow long-day storage onion; standard cooking onion. Selections include Yellow Globe Danvers, Southport.
Short-day flat red Louisiana heirloom; pungent, stores longer than most short-day types. Deep South choice for a storage onion.

alliums.source: Cornell Garlic Crop Profile; The Garlic Seed Foundation
alliums.notes: Porcelain hardneck strain; large 4 to 6 clove white bulbs, strong long-keeping garlic. The Northeast / Midwest standard.

alliums.source: Cornell Garlic Crop Profile; The Garlic Seed Foundation
alliums.notes: Rocambole hardneck heirloom (1900s NY immigration); 8 to 10 large brown-purple cloves, classic robust garlic flavor. Short shelf life (3 to 4 months).

alliums.source: USDA-ARS Allium sativum collection; The Garlic Seed Foundation
alliums.notes: Glazed Purple Stripe hardneck; metallic purple skin, sweet roasted flavor. Cold-hardy.

alliums.source: USDA-ARS Plant Genetic Resources Unit collection records; The Garlic Seed Foundation
alliums.notes: Republic of Georgia Purple Stripe hardneck; widely planted, complex sweet roasted flavor. 9 to 10 cloves per bulb.

alliums.source: OSU Extension; The Garlic Seed Foundation
alliums.notes: Botanically a leek (Allium ampeloprasum), not garlic; huge 4 to 6 oz bulbs, mild garlic-onion flavor. 4 to 6 large cloves.

alliums.source: Texas A&M AgriLife Extension; The Garlic Seed Foundation southern garlic notes
alliums.notes: Creole softneck for warm climates (mild winter zones); deep red skin, small bulbs, intense flavor, excellent storage. The Deep South / Gulf Coast / desert SW choice.

alliums.source: Seed Savers Exchange; The Garlic Seed Foundation; Rodale Institute garlic taste trials
alliums.notes: Colville Reservation WA softneck; Rodale Press taste-test winner. 8 to 20 cloves per bulb, mild creamy flavor, excellent 9-month storage.

alliums.source: Cornell Garlic Crop Profile; The Garlic Seed Foundation
alliums.notes: Silverskin softneck; tightest braiding cultivar, very long storage (up to 12 months), papery silver skin.

alliums.source: Seed Savers Exchange; The Garlic Seed Foundation
alliums.notes: Artichoke softneck; the canonical Italian cooking garlic. 12 to 20 cloves in concentric rows, rich flavor, 8+ month storage.

alliums.source: USDA NRCS Plant Guide Allium cernuum
alliums.notes: Native onion of PNW prairies; mild garlicky-onion flavor; pink umbels are pollinator favorites and edible. Distinct from camas by its onion smell.
Pacific coast native onion of dry meadows and serpentine; small white umbels; bulbs traditionally roasted by Indigenous peoples.
Short-day sweet yellow onion for southern gardens, latitudes below 35N. Mild, juicy, fall planting in zones 8 to 10.
Short-day white onion for southern gardens below 35N latitude. Mild flavor, eat fresh.
Short-day sweet onion, parent of Vidalia. Flat round bulbs, plant in latitudes below 35N.
Intermediate-day sweet yellow hybrid. Adapts across most of US (latitudes 32 to 42N).
Intermediate-day red onion, broadly adaptable. Mild flavor, good fresh-eating.
Intermediate-day deep red storage onion. Holds color through winter storage.
Long elongated red onion, mild and sweet. Intermediate-day for mid-latitudes.
Long-day storage yellow onion. Firm, pungent, exceptional keeper.

alliums.source: Baker Creek catalog descriptor; Seed Savers Exchange yearbook
alliums.notes: Scottish heirloom long-day yellow exhibition onion. Huge mild globes, eat fresh.
Long-day large mild yellow globe. Reliable across northern gardens above 38N.
Long-day large mild white globe. Best for fresh eating, shorter storage than yellows.
Long-day red storage onion. Holds color, classic northern winter keeper.
Long-day yellow storage onion. Tight necks for excellent winter storage.
Long-day yellow storage hybrid. Uniform globes, productive in northern fields.
German long-day flat yellow storage onion. Classic for sets, excellent keeper.
Long-day red storage onion. Round bulbs, pungent and dependable.
Intermediate-day early red onion. Quick to maturity, mild flavor.
Italian flat golden cipollini-type onion. Sweet, classic for roasting whole.
Rocambole hardneck, mahogany-blushed wrappers. Rich complex flavor, 4 to 6 month storage.
Purple stripe hardneck from Uzbekistan. Mild warm flavor, 8 to 12 cloves per bulb.
Porcelain hardneck, very hot intense flavor. 4 to 6 large cloves per bulb.
Rocambole hardneck with rich full flavor. Cold-hardy, popular in Pacific Northwest.
Turban-type hardneck from Korea. Early-maturing, hot raw flavor, mellows when cooked.
Asiatic hardneck from Korea. Early, hot raw flavor, vibrant purple wrappers.
Asiatic hardneck from North Korea. Early summer harvest, sharp pungent flavor.
Asiatic hardneck from Korea. Plump cloves, mild when cooked, hot when raw.
Rocambole-type hardneck, rich full flavor. Cold-hardy and productive.
Rocambole hardneck from the Carpathian mountains. Strong flavor, easy to peel.
Marbled purple stripe from Belarus. Very cold-hardy, rich complex flavor.
Rocambole hardneck, hot rich flavor. 6 to 12 cloves per bulb, easy to peel.
Silverskin softneck, mild flavor, stores 9 to 12 months. Good for braiding.
Artichoke softneck with reddish wrappers. Mild flavor, long storage.
Porcelain hardneck from Oregon. Very large bulbs, robust flavor.
Asiatic hardneck, brown-streaked wrappers. Early harvest, hot raw flavor.
Creole softneck with deep red wrappers. Adapts to warm-winter climates, sweet flavor.
Spanish creole softneck. Heat-tolerant, sweet mild flavor, good for southern gardens.
Creole softneck for warm climates. Sweet rich flavor, productive in mild winters.
Artichoke softneck. White wrappers, long storage, easy for braiding.
Commercial artichoke softneck. Long storage, white wrappers, mild flavor.
Early-maturing artichoke softneck. Mild flavor, commercial garlic standard.
Artichoke softneck, mild flavor. Reliable in cool mountain climates.

alliums.source: Filaree Garlic Farm descriptor; Seed Savers Exchange yearbook
alliums.notes: Artichoke softneck, brought to Washington by Lorz family. Strong robust flavor, long storage.
Silverskin softneck from Washington's Nootka Island. Rose-tinged wrappers, long storage.
Long-day red onion with red rings throughout. Stores 6 months, holds color.
Italian red top-shaped storage onion. Intermediate to long-day, traditional for braiding.
White flat Italian cipollini-type onion. Sweet roasted, classic agrodolce ingredient.
Italian red flat onion, sweet and mild. Intermediate-day, eat fresh.
Italian elongated red onion, similar to Tropea. Sweet and tender.
Italian purple-tinged artichoke softneck. Long storage, mild flavor.
Marbled purple stripe hardneck from Republic of Georgia. Hot pungent flavor, large cloves.
Marbled purple stripe hardneck. Extremely cold-hardy, mild rich flavor.
Glazed purple stripe hardneck from Armenia. Beautiful glazed wrappers, complex flavor.
Porcelain hardneck. Mild for porcelain types, sweet when roasted.
Porcelain hardneck from Georgia. Hot pungent flavor, large bulbs.
Porcelain hardneck, very cold-hardy. Large pearl-white cloves, robust flavor.
Spanish silverskin softneck. Mild flavor, very long storage, good for braiding.
Czech porcelain hardneck. Large bulbs, hot pungent flavor when fresh.
Turban hardneck from Central Asia. Early-maturing, beautiful purple-streaked bulbs.
Long-day pink storage onion. Pretty for the table, stores 4 to 6 months.
Open-pollinated long-day yellow storage onion from Dakotas. Excellent winter keeper.
Long-day yellow storage onion. Tight tops cure well for very long storage.
Long-day yellow storage hybrid. Pungent flavor, excellent winter keeper.
Long-day red storage onion. Holds color and quality through winter.
Spanish purple stripe hardneck. Rich complex flavor, beautiful purple-streaked bulbs.
Pink-tinted porcelain hardneck selection of Music. Cold-hardy, very large cloves.
Glazed purple stripe hardneck. Rich earthy flavor, beautiful glazed bulbs.
Czech porcelain hardneck. Very large bulbs, robust hot flavor.
Russian Asiatic hardneck. Early harvest, hot raw flavor.
Artichoke softneck. Mild flavor, very long storage, classic for braiding.
Italian red flat onion from Piacenza. Sweet and mild, intermediate-day.
1880s English long-day exhibition onion. Huge mild yellow globes, eat fresh.
Italian rose-colored sweet onion. Intermediate-day, eat fresh.
Pre-1873 Connecticut long-day white onion. Round globes, eat fresh or short storage.
Long-day deep red globe heirloom. Stores 4 to 6 months, classic for kitchens.
Pennsylvania Amish heritage rocambole hardneck. Rich complex flavor, easy to peel.
Italian artichoke softneck with purple-streaked wrappers. Mild flavor, long storage.
Porcelain hardneck from Romania. Hot pungent flavor, very large bulbs.
Heirloom strain with potato-like underground bulbs and topsets. Use bulbs like shallot; tops like scallion.
Pennsylvania heirloom. Forms secondary tier of topsets on top of primary topsets. Vigorous and cold-hardy.
Southern heirloom. White-bulbed type; milder than yellow walking onions. Heat tolerant.

alliums.source: USDA GRIN germplasm descriptor; Fedco Seeds
alliums.notes: Perennial scallion. Clump-forming; never bulbs. Tender green tops cut-and-come-again across seasons.

lettuce.notes: Romaine and butterhead are easier than crisphead in home gardens.
Upright heads more heat-tolerant than butterhead. Parris Island, Jericho, Forellenschluss.
Soft loose heads; Boston, Bibb, Buttercrunch. Bolts in heat - shade in summer.
Crisphead; needs cool steady weather to form tight heads. Difficult in hot summers.
Crispheads of European origin; bolt-resistant, good for hot summers (Nevada, Sierra).
AAS winner; reliable bibb-type with thick juicy leaves. More heat-tolerant than typical butterheads.
AAS winner; bronze-red ruffled looseleaf. Slow-bolting - one of the best for hot summers.
Mini romaine; 6-inch single-serving heads. Closely spaced (4-6 in) for high yield per bed.
Italian intensely-frilled looseleaf; deep red. Mild flavor, holds shape on the plate. Lollo Bionda is the green sister cultivar.
1840s American heirloom; pointed elongated leaves. Heat-tolerant, slow to bolt. Available in green and red (Red Deer Tongue, Matchless).
Austrian heirloom romaine; green leaves splashed with maroon ('speckled trout back' is the name's literal meaning).
USDA-developed romaine; the workhorse romaine cultivar for commercial and home use. 12-inch upright heads.
1885 French heirloom butterhead; cold-hardy, plantable spring through fall. Red-tipped green leaves.
Romaine-type heirloom; green leaves with maroon speckles. Bolt-resistant, similar to Forellenschluss but tighter heads.
Looseleaf heirloom; bronze-tipped pointed leaves. Mild, slow-bolting, good for warm-summer gardens.
French heirloom batavian crisphead; red-blushed leaves, slow to bolt in heat. Excellent for summer salads.
1840 French heirloom; cold-hardy red romaine for overwintering. Develops deepest color in cool weather.

lettuce.source: All-America Selections 1963 records; Cornell vegetable variety database
lettuce.notes: AAS 1963 butterhead winner; loose dark green head, slow to bolt, the durable home-garden butterhead.

lettuce.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Johnny's Selected Seeds bolt-resistance trials
lettuce.notes: Batavian / summer crisp lettuces (Nevada, Magenta, Sierra); crisp like iceberg, bolt-resistant like butterhead. The summer lettuce pick.

lettuce.source: Seed Savers Exchange; Cornell vegetable variety database
lettuce.notes: American heirloom (Matchless); tongue-shaped pointed loose-leaf lettuce, green and red strains. Heat-tolerant.
AAS 1985 winner; bronze-red ruffled loose-leaf, slow to bolt, the durable colored loose-leaf.

lettuce.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds variety notes; Cornell vegetable variety database
lettuce.notes: Wine-red glossy loose-leaf; the darkest red lettuce in common circulation. Holds color well in heat.

lettuce.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds; Royal Horticultural Society lettuce trials
lettuce.notes: British mini-romaine; 5 to 6 inch upright single-serving heads. Sweet, crisp, the gold-standard small lettuce. 12 inch spacing or tighter.

lettuce.source: Seed Savers Exchange; Cornell vegetable variety database
lettuce.notes: 1850s English heirloom mini butterhead; tennis-ball sized heads on tight 6 inch spacing. Excellent container or windowsill crop.

lettuce.source: Seed Savers Exchange; Austrian heritage seed collections
lettuce.notes: Austrian heirloom (the name means trout-back); romaine with red speckles on green leaves. Same family as Flashy Trout's Back.

lettuce.source: Seed Savers Exchange; University of Kentucky Cooperative Extension
lettuce.notes: Kentucky 1865 Major John Bibb release; small butterhead, the bibb-type namesake. Tender, sweet, slightly cupped leaves.

lettuce.source: Michigan State University vegetable variety records; Cornell vegetable database
lettuce.notes: 1890s MI greenhouse loose-leaf; the original greenhouse / cool-temperature lettuce. Frilly green leaves, very fast.

lettuce.source: Seed Savers Exchange; UC ANR overwinter lettuce notes
lettuce.notes: 1840s French winter romaine; flat red-tinged heads, cold-hardy through mild winters. Excellent overwinter lettuce in Mediterranean and maritime climates.

lettuce.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds; Royal Horticultural Society lettuce notes
lettuce.notes: British heirloom cos-butterhead cross; cold hardy, the standard overwinter lettuce under low tunnels in Zones 6 to 8.

lettuce.source: Kitazawa Seed Company variety notes; Cornell vegetable variety database
lettuce.notes: Chinese stem-lettuce; bolting variety grown for the thick juicy stem (peeled, sliced raw or stir-fried). Crisp celery-asparagus-cucumber flavor. The defining vegetable of woasun.

lettuce.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds catalog descriptor; Seed Savers Exchange yearbook
lettuce.notes: Classic butterhead with soft, loose heads and buttery flavor. Bolts in heat, best in spring and fall.

lettuce.source: Baker Creek catalog descriptor; Seed Savers Exchange yearbook
lettuce.notes: French heirloom butterhead, rose-tinged leaves with green hearts. Adaptable across seasons, cold-hardy with cover.
Bronze-red butterhead from the Balkans. Slow to bolt, holds well in warm spring weather.
French heirloom butterhead splashed with deep red. Tender, mild flavor.
Small red bibb-type butterhead. Compact heads sized for single-serving harvest.
Classic upright green romaine. Heat-tolerant and tip-burn resistant, reliable in market gardens.

lettuce.source: Baker Creek catalog descriptor; Seed Savers Exchange yearbook
lettuce.notes: Deep red romaine, 1700s heirloom. Holds color in heat, slow to bolt.
Polish heirloom looseleaf with rosy-purple leaves. Mild, crunchy, holds well in cool weather.
English heirloom romaine, dense upright heads with crisp ribs. Vigorous and reliable.
Russian-origin red romaine. Cold-tolerant, overwinters under cover in zones 6 and warmer.
Hawaiian-bred summer crisp, slow to bolt in heat. Excellent for southern and tropical gardens.
AAS winner, deeply lobed green oakleaf. Cut-and-come-again for weeks.
Bronze-red oakleaf, vigorous and slow to bolt. Beautiful in salad mixes.
French red oakleaf with bronze tips. Elegant for restaurant salads.
Pre-1900 French heirloom butterhead-oakleaf type with bronzed leaves. Compact and heat-tolerant.
English crisphead heirloom, large frilled heads. Slow to bolt for a crisphead.

lettuce.source: Baker Creek catalog descriptor; Seed Savers Exchange yearbook
lettuce.notes: French ice queen, batavian-crisphead crossover. Deeply notched leaves form crunchy heads.
Red summer crisp, heat-tolerant batavian. Stays crunchy and sweet through warm weather.
Green summer-crisp batavian, bolt-resistant in heat. Reliable hot-weather lettuce.
Red-tinged batavian summer crisp. Heat-tolerant, holds in field without bolting.
Classic iceberg-type crisphead, 1944 AAS winner. Needs cool growing window for solid heads.
Early adaptable iceberg, holds in heat better than most crispheads.
Pre-1850 American looseleaf, light-green crinkled leaves. Fast and dependable for cut-and-come-again.
Italian frilly red looseleaf. Dramatic ruffled leaves for salad mixes.
Italian frilly green looseleaf, the green sister to Lollo Rossa.
Chartreuse-leafed looseleaf, slow to bolt and heat-tolerant.
1958 AAS winner, deep red leaf lettuce. Color deepens in cool weather.
Deeply cut frilly green looseleaf, endive-like form. Tangy flavor, excellent in mesclun.
Green frilly leaf, named for bolt resistance. Reliable summer leaf lettuce.
Amish heirloom bibb butterhead splashed with red flecks. Tender, mild, beautiful.
Red summer-crisp batavian. Heat-tolerant for southern gardens.
Green summer-crisp Batavian. Slow bolting, sweet flavor through heat.
Deep burgundy oakleaf, near-black foliage. Striking in salad mixes.
English heirloom butterhead with red-tinged leaves. Tender, Victorian-era cultivar.

lettuce.source: Baker Creek catalog descriptor; Seed Savers Exchange yearbook
lettuce.notes: Italian heirloom looseleaf with frilled red-tipped leaves. Lush, ornamental, slow to bolt.
French heirloom four-season butterhead with bronze tinted leaves. Tender and cold-tolerant.
Austrian heirloom romaine, green with maroon speckles. Striking in salads.
Israeli desert romaine, heat- and bolt-resistant. Stands well in southern summer.
Compact buttercrunch for tight spacing or containers. Plate-sized heads.
Red-tinged crisphead, novelty form of iceberg. Crunchy with color.
Pennsylvania Amish heirloom, pointed deer-tongue shaped leaves. Bolt-resistant.
Red-tinted form of Deer Tongue. Bronze-red triangular leaves, slow to bolt.
German bronze-tipped butterhead. Heat-tolerant for late spring sowings.
Deep red butterhead, slow to bolt. Heat-tolerant and disease-resistant.
Standard green romaine, parent line for many modern types. Reliable, productive.
Large green heat-tolerant romaine. Slow to bolt, good for southern markets.
Original French batavian Ice Queen. Sharply notched green leaves form crunchy head.
Pre-1804 American butterhead grown by Thomas Jefferson. Small dense heads.
French red looseleaf. Bronze-tipped, slow to bolt, classic for mesclun.
Tall green romaine, upright heads. Resists tipburn, productive for markets.
Improved Forellenschluss-type green and red speckled romaine. Compact upright heads.
Bred for greenhouse and high-tunnel production. Five-leaf-type blend for baby leaf.
Multi-cultivar baby leaf blend. Cut-and-come-again, sized for mesclun mix.
French batavian-type with red tinged crisp leaves. Cold-hardy for overwintering.
Lobed dark-green lettuce with mustard-like form. Cut-and-come-again, bolt-resistant.
French batavian with red-tipped green leaves. Heat-tolerant, holds in summer.
Turkish heirloom green romaine from Istanbul. Heat-tolerant, classic Mediterranean type.

endive.notes: Broad-leaved relative of endive. Less bitter; great in soups.
Cool weather sweetens the bitterness. Heads tighten with cold nights.
Two-stage crop: grow root in field summer, force in dark sand/cellar in winter for 'chicons' (white tight 6-inch heads). Bitter-sweet salad green.
Italian Roman classic; hollow asparagus-like flowering shoots cut from rosette. Slice thin, soak in ice water for the famous Roman salad.

spinach.notes: Heat-loving spinach substitute. Climbing vine, needs trellis.
Tendergreen; mild mustard flavor, more cold-hardy than komatsuna.
Tropical aquatic green; cut-and-come-again. FEDERAL NOXIOUS WEED in US, illegal to plant or transport without USDA permit. Check state restrictions.
1925 American heirloom; deeply savoyed (crinkled) dark-green leaves, sweet flavor. Long Standing Bloomsdale is bolt-resistant strain.
Vine layer heat-loving climber. Red-stemmed cultivar, edible glossy leaves (mucilaginous spinach substitute). Tropical perennial, US annual. Trellis.
Vine layer green-stemmed Basella. Cut and come again spinach substitute. Tolerates 90F+ when true spinach bolts. South Asian staple. Trellis.

spinach.source: Kitazawa Seed; USDA Federal Noxious Weed List
spinach.notes: Aquatic and herb layer. Edible hollow stems and leaves, SE Asian stir fry staple. Wet bed or flooded paddy. INVASIVE noxious in many US states.
Maya staple leafy green; MUST be cooked at least 5 minutes to inactivate cyanogenic glycosides; not eaten raw. Sweeter cultivars Estrella and Chayamansa are spineless and preferred.

spinach.source: Univ of Florida IFAS Gynura procumbens
spinach.notes: SE Asian perennial leafy green; mild succulent leaves with grassy-nutty flavor; eaten fresh or in stir-fries. Very productive cut-and-come-again in subtropical climates.

spinach.source: UF/IFAS tropical greens notes; Kitazawa Seed Company
spinach.notes: Tropical climbing vine with red-purple stems and thick succulent leaves; heat-tolerant spinach substitute. Productive once warm; the summer spinach.

spinach.source: USDA APHIS noxious weed list; Kitazawa Seed Company variety notes
spinach.notes: Southeast Asian semiaquatic green; the defining vegetable of pad pak boong and rau muong. Federally listed as a noxious weed in most US states; check local rules.

tomatoes.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Tomato Production
tomatoes.notes: Faster than slicers; ideal for short-season gardens. Indeterminate varieties need staking. Sungold, Sweet 100, Black Cherry are common cultivars.

tomatoes.source: WSU Extension - Tomato Cultivars for Washington
tomatoes.notes: Roma, San Marzano, Amish Paste types. Determinate growth - concentrated harvest for canning. Less prone to cracking than slicers.

tomatoes.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Tomato Production
tomatoes.notes: Large fruit needs full season - skip in zones 3-4 unless using high tunnels. Brandywine, Cherokee Purple, Mortgage Lifter are heirloom favorites.

tomatoes.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Tomato Production
tomatoes.notes: Oblong, denser flesh than cherry; less prone to cracking. Juliet and Santa F1 are reliable performers.

tomatoes.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Heirloom Tomato Cultivars
tomatoes.notes: Amish heirloom; potato-leaf foliage, large pink fruit with classic tomato flavor. Sudduth's Strain is the most flavorful. Skip in zones 3-4 unless using a high tunnel.

tomatoes.source: UT Extension - Tomato Cultivars for Tennessee
tomatoes.notes: Tennessee heirloom said to come from the Cherokee people; dusky purple-pink fruit, smoky sweet flavor. Crack-prone in heavy rain - mulch and water consistently.

tomatoes.source: UC ANR - Paste Tomato Production
tomatoes.notes: Italian DOP paste tomato; long thin fruit, dense low-water flesh, the canonical Neapolitan pizza-sauce tomato. Indeterminate - stake or cage.

tomatoes.source: Penn State Extension - Paste Tomato Production
tomatoes.notes: Determinate paste tomato; concentrated harvest for canning. More disease-tolerant than San Marzano and easier in humid summers.

tomatoes.source: OSU Extension Service - Specialty Tomatoes
tomatoes.notes: Tom Wagner cultivar; ripe at green-with-yellow-stripes (tang-sweet flavor). Looks unripe even when mature - go by gentle squeeze.

tomatoes.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Heirloom Tomato Cultivars
tomatoes.notes: Russian (Crimean) heirloom; dark mahogany-purple fruit, salty-rich flavor. More heat-tolerant than most beefsteaks.

tomatoes.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Heirloom Tomato Cultivars
tomatoes.notes: 1930s West Virginia heirloom by M.C. Byles (paid off his mortgage selling seedlings). 1-3 lb pink fruit, mild sweet flavor. Heavy yielder.

tomatoes.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Heirloom Tomato Cultivars
tomatoes.notes: Wisconsin heirloom; oxheart-shaped paste tomato, sweeter and meatier than Roma. Indeterminate - long harvest window. Excellent for sauce and dehydrating.

tomatoes.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Heirloom Tomato Cultivars
tomatoes.notes: West Virginia heirloom; yellow-with-red-streaks 1-2 lb beefsteak, low acid, sweet melon-fruit flavor.

tomatoes.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Heirloom Tomato Cultivars
tomatoes.notes: Michigan heirloom; bright orange 1-2 lb beefsteak, low acid, mango-citrus undertones. Color matches a glass of orange juice.

tomatoes.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Cherry Tomato Cultivars
tomatoes.notes: F1 cherry hybrid; intense tropical-fruit sweetness, the gold standard of cherry tomato flavor. Skin cracks when over-ripe - pick daily.

tomatoes.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Tomato Cultivars
tomatoes.notes: AAS-winning grape tomato hybrid; crack-resistant, disease-tolerant, very productive. Reliable workhorse for humid eastern gardens.

tomatoes.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Heirloom Tomatoes
tomatoes.notes: 1700s European heirloom; small pear-shaped yellow fruit. Mild flavor; very productive vines through frost.

tomatoes.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Heirloom Tomatoes
tomatoes.notes: British heirloom cherry; sweet-tart balance, crack-resistant. Trusses of 6-12 fruit; very heavy yields.

tomatoes.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Cherry Tomato Cultivars
tomatoes.notes: Modern selection; dark mahogany-purple cherries with rich complex flavor. Heat-tolerant.

tomatoes.source: UC ANR; Petoseed/Seminis cultivar trials; The Old Farmer's Almanac
tomatoes.notes: Workhorse early indeterminate; first ripe fruit weeks ahead of beefsteaks. Crack-prone if watering swings.

tomatoes.source: Burpee cultivar release records; Cornell Cooperative Extension home tomato guide
tomatoes.notes: Classic indeterminate slicer; VFN disease resistance package makes it forgiving for new gardeners.

tomatoes.source: All-America Selections winner records; Texas A&M AgriLife Extension
tomatoes.notes: All-America Selections 1984 winner; determinate so fruit ripens in a concentrated window. Excellent disease resistance (VFFNTA).

tomatoes.source: Rutgers New Jersey Agricultural Experiment Station cultivar release notes
tomatoes.notes: 1934 Rutgers University and Campbell Soup release; the canning and paste standard for decades. Determinate, balanced sweet/acid.

tomatoes.source: Seed Savers Exchange catalog; OSU Extension northern-grower tomato variety notes
tomatoes.notes: Czech heirloom; sets fruit in cool soil where heat-loving cultivars sulk. Potato-leaf, indeterminate, golf-ball-sized fruits.

tomatoes.source: Seed Savers Exchange; Washington State University Extension cool-season tomato evaluation
tomatoes.notes: Swedish breeder release; semi-determinate, prolific in cool maritime and short northern summers. 2 to 3 oz fruits.

tomatoes.source: University of Alaska Fairbanks Cooperative Extension; Seed Savers Exchange
tomatoes.notes: Russian heirloom; one of the few tomatoes that sets fruit in genuinely cold nights. Determinate, 3 to 5 oz fruit.

tomatoes.source: Agriculture and Agri-Food Canada Beaverlodge Research Farm cultivar release records
tomatoes.notes: Beaverlodge AB Research Station release for the Canadian prairie; arguably the earliest open-pollinated tomato available. Compact determinate, 2 oz cherries clustered heavily.

tomatoes.source: Seed Savers Exchange; SSE 2008 trial notes
tomatoes.notes: Russian heirloom despite the name; mahogany-purple pear shape, smoky flavor. Indeterminate. Crack-resistant for a black tomato.

tomatoes.source: University of California Vegetable Research and Information Center; Italian seed-saver collections
tomatoes.notes: Italian heirloom; deeply ribbed, lobed, almost pleated red beefsteak. Heat tolerant, performs well in long Mediterranean summers. Indeterminate.

tomatoes.source: Seed Savers Exchange; Cornell University tomato evaluation
tomatoes.notes: Mexican Hidalgo wild cerasiforme line; tiny half-inch red cherries with intense old-tomato flavor. Indeterminate, disease-resistant, prolific to a fault.

tomatoes.source: Seed Savers Exchange; Italian heirloom seed catalogs
tomatoes.notes: Italian heirloom drying tomato; small plum-shaped 1.5 oz fruits in heavy clusters. Determinate. Low moisture content makes it the classic sun-dried tomato.

tomatoes.source: All-America Selections 1943 winner records
tomatoes.notes: AAS 1943 winner; the original yellow-orange slicer that mainstreamed non-red tomatoes. Indeterminate, low-acid, mild flavor.

tomatoes.source: Seed Savers Exchange; Tomato Growers Supply variety notes
tomatoes.notes: Belgian breeder Pascal Moreau release; green-purple shouldered beefsteak with marbled rainbow flesh. Indeterminate.

tomatoes.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds variety notes; Cornell vegetable variety database
tomatoes.notes: Compact bright-yellow determinate; 4 oz round fruits, mild flavor. Good container choice. AAS-style early yellow.

tomatoes.source: Harris Seeds cultivar release notes; Cornell Cooperative Extension
tomatoes.notes: Harris Seeds 1969 hybrid; indeterminate red slicer, low-acid, crack-resistant. Long the Northeast home-garden default.

tomatoes.source: NC State University Mountain Horticultural Crops Research Station release records
tomatoes.notes: NCSU Randy Gardner release; campari-type 2 oz red cocktail tomato with late-blight resistance. Indeterminate, prolific. Excellent for the rainy Northeast/Appalachia.

tomatoes.source: Seed Savers Exchange; Tomato Growers Supply variety notes
tomatoes.notes: Hungarian / Russian heirloom; large 1 to 2 lb heart-shaped pink-red beefsteak. Indeterminate, low seed count, dense meaty flesh ideal for fresh eating.

tomatoes.source: Seed Savers Exchange; SSE 1992 trial notes
tomatoes.notes: Early Brandywine-type pink heirloom; potato-leaf indeterminate. Brandywine flavor in a shorter season; good Northeast pick.

tomatoes.source: Seed Savers Exchange; Heirloom Tomatoes Carolyn Male reference
tomatoes.notes: Yellow potato-leaf sibling of pink Brandywine; sweet, slightly tropical, large lobed beefsteak. Indeterminate.

tomatoes.source: Seed Savers Exchange; Native Seeds/SEARCH
tomatoes.notes: Oaxaca Mexico heirloom; yellow-orange bicolor beefsteak streaked with red. 1 to 2 lb, indeterminate, low acid sweet.

tomatoes.source: Seed Savers Exchange; University of Minnesota Extension
tomatoes.notes: Minnesota bicolor heirloom; yellow with red interior streaks, 1 to 2 lb. Indeterminate, prolific.

tomatoes.source: Seed Savers Exchange; Heritage Harvest Festival tasting notes
tomatoes.notes: Russian dark-shoulder heirloom; brick-red with green-bronze shoulders, smoky sweet flavor. Indeterminate. Beloved at heirloom tomato tastings.

tomatoes.source: Seed Savers Exchange; SSE 1991 yearbook
tomatoes.notes: Greene County Tennessee German-American heirloom; large beefsteak that stays green at maturity with a pink-yellow blush. Sweet spicy flavor, indeterminate. SSE distributed 1991.

tomatoes.source: Seed Savers Exchange yearbook
tomatoes.notes: WV German-American heirloom; yellow with pink streaks, 1 lb beefsteak. Mild, fruity, low acid. Indeterminate.

tomatoes.source: Seed Savers Exchange; Carl Swidorski / Polish-American heritage seed collection
tomatoes.notes: Polish heirloom paste; long banana-shaped 4 to 6 inch fruit, dry meaty flesh, ideal for sauce. Indeterminate, wispy potato leaves; cage early.

tomatoes.source: Seed Savers Exchange Eva Purple Ball
tomatoes.notes: Black Forest German heirloom brought to Mississippi 1800s; round 5oz dusty purple-pink fruits with rich complex flavor; crack and disease resistant in humid climates.

tomatoes.source: Univ of Arkansas Cooperative Extension MP-265
tomatoes.notes: Pre-1900 Arkansas heirloom; medium pink 6 to 8 oz fruits; crack and disease resistant; mild sweet flavor under high heat where many tomatoes fail to set fruit.

tomatoes.source: LSU AgCenter Creole tomato release
tomatoes.notes: LSU AgCenter 1969 release; deep red 6 to 8 oz fruits with high acid balance; disease resistance including Fusarium wilt races 1 and 2. Iconic Mississippi Delta variety.

tomatoes.source: Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds (Rare Seeds) catalog descriptor; Seed Savers Exchange yearbook
tomatoes.notes: Indeterminate black-purple beefsteak, 10-12 oz fruit, smoky-sweet flavor. Won the 2005 Heirloom Garden Show taste-off. Cage for vigorous vines.

tomatoes.source: Seed Savers Exchange yearbook; Fedco Seeds catalog descriptor
tomatoes.notes: Ukrainian heirloom honoring cosmonaut Vladislav Volkov. 8-12 oz red globes, balanced acid-sweet. Cool-tolerant, productive in short seasons.

tomatoes.source: Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds (Rare Seeds) catalog descriptor
tomatoes.notes: Determinate pale-yellow paste, 3 inch sausage shape. Low acid, mild sweet flavor; rare yellow paste useful for two-color sauces.

tomatoes.source: Seed Savers Exchange yearbook; Fedco Seeds catalog descriptor
tomatoes.notes: Bicolor yellow-red beefsteak, 1-2 lb fruit with marbled cross-section. Complex sweet-fruity flavor; rich slicer for showcase plates.

tomatoes.source: Seed Savers Exchange yearbook; Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds catalog descriptor
tomatoes.notes: Mahogany-brown sport of Cherokee Purple from Craig LeHoullier. 12 oz fruit, sweeter than the parent. Crack-resistant; mulch for steady moisture.

tomatoes.source: Seed Savers Exchange yearbook; Fedco Seeds catalog descriptor
tomatoes.notes: Classic pink Brandywine line, potato-leaf, 1 lb fruit. Indeterminate; stake heavily. Skip zones 3-4 without a tunnel.

tomatoes.source: Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds (Rare Seeds) catalog descriptor
tomatoes.notes: Red-fruited Brandywine variant, regular leaf. Slightly earlier than pink Brandywine, equally flavorful. Heavy feeder.

tomatoes.source: NC State Extension heirloom tomato bulletin; Seed Savers Exchange yearbook
tomatoes.notes: North Carolina pink heirloom, 1 lb fruit, low acid. Reputed parent of Mortgage Lifter. Tolerates humid Southeast summers.

tomatoes.source: Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds (Rare Seeds) catalog descriptor
tomatoes.notes: Yellow beefsteak with red striping, sweet mild flavor. Catfacing common in cool springs; pick a sheltered spot.

tomatoes.source: Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds (Rare Seeds) catalog descriptor
tomatoes.notes: Cream-white beefsteak, 8 oz, very low acid. Looks ivory when ripe. Pair with reds for striking salad plates.

tomatoes.source: Seed Savers Exchange yearbook; Fedco Seeds catalog descriptor
tomatoes.notes: Green-when-ripe cherry from breeder Dr. Carolyn Male, intensely sweet. Pick when soft with amber blush, not by color.

tomatoes.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds variety guide
tomatoes.notes: Hybrid lemon-yellow globe, 7 oz, mild-sweet. VFN resistance; reliable across zones. Indeterminate, cage early.

tomatoes.source: Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds (Rare Seeds) catalog descriptor; Seed Savers Exchange yearbook
tomatoes.notes: Orange beefsteak from Russia via Thomas Jefferson's records, 12-16 oz. Meaty, tropical-fruity flavor. Crack-prone if watering swings.

tomatoes.source: Wild Boar Farms catalog; Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds (Rare Seeds) descriptor
tomatoes.notes: Brad Gates breeding, pink with green and yellow stripes, 8-12 oz. Strong sweet-tart balance. Striking on market tables.

tomatoes.source: Fedco Seeds catalog descriptor; Seed Savers Exchange yearbook
tomatoes.notes: British heirloom, red with gold stripes, 2 inch fruit. Very early, productive in cool summers. Pick at first stripe color.

tomatoes.source: Wild Boar Farms catalog; Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds descriptor
tomatoes.notes: Pink cherry with yellow stripes from Wild Boar Farms, very sweet. Crack-resistant; productive trusses.

tomatoes.source: Wild Boar Farms catalog; Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds descriptor
tomatoes.notes: Gold-orange cherry with red marbling, tropical-fruity. Wild Boar Farms; one of the showiest cherries.

tomatoes.source: Seed Savers Exchange yearbook; Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds descriptor
tomatoes.notes: Fuzzy yellow-pink 2 oz fruit, mild sweet flavor, peach-like skin. Stores 3-4 weeks at room temperature, longest of any tomato.

tomatoes.source: Seed Savers Exchange yearbook; Fedco Seeds catalog descriptor
tomatoes.notes: Pennsylvania Dutch heirloom (Giant Bunch of Grapes), 1 oz red cherries in 20-40 fruit trusses. Rich full-tomato flavor.

tomatoes.source: Seed Savers Exchange yearbook; Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds descriptor
tomatoes.notes: Russian heirloom, mahogany 2 inch plum, rich smoky flavor. Great fresh or oven-roasted.

tomatoes.source: Seed Savers Exchange yearbook; Fedco Seeds catalog descriptor
tomatoes.notes: John Swenson's red-and-gold striped paste, 5 inch fruit. Meaty, low juice, excellent for sauce and drying.

tomatoes.source: Seed Savers Exchange yearbook; Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds descriptor
tomatoes.notes: Red 10-16 oz fruit, crack-resistant, prolific. Old standard for canning; reliable in mid-Atlantic and Midwest.

tomatoes.source: USDA cultivar history; Seed Savers Exchange yearbook
tomatoes.notes: 1925 USDA release, determinate red globe, 6 oz. Disease-resistant historical parent of many modern cultivars.

tomatoes.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds variety guide
tomatoes.notes: Tougher-skinned sibling of Sungold, golden-orange cherry, very high Brix. Less prone to splitting than Sungold.

tomatoes.source: Seed Savers Exchange yearbook; Fedco Seeds catalog descriptor
tomatoes.notes: 1 lb red beefsteak, very few seeds, low juice. Dual-purpose slicer and sauce. Strong plants, heavy yield.

tomatoes.source: Seed Savers Exchange yearbook; Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds descriptor
tomatoes.notes: Russian determinate, mahogany 6-8 oz fruit, potato-leaf. Earliest of the black tomatoes; good for short seasons.

tomatoes.source: Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds (Rare Seeds) catalog descriptor
tomatoes.notes: New Zealand heirloom orange globe, 6 oz, sweet-tangy. Brought to England by the 6th Earl. Reliable producer.

tomatoes.source: Seed Savers Exchange yearbook; Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds descriptor
tomatoes.notes: Tennessee heirloom, late, 1 lb yellow beefsteak, potato-leaf. Considered one of the finest-tasting yellows.

tomatoes.source: Seed Savers Exchange yearbook; Fedco Seeds catalog descriptor
tomatoes.notes: Russian heart-shaped pink, 1 lb, juicy and sweet. Early for an oxheart; good northern slicer.

tomatoes.source: Seed Savers Exchange yearbook
tomatoes.notes: Bavarian heirloom that helped launch Seed Savers Exchange. 1-2 lb pink beefsteak, low acid, meaty.

tomatoes.source: Seed Savers Exchange yearbook; Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds descriptor
tomatoes.notes: Sausage-shaped red paste, 8-12 oz. Few seeds, dense flesh, top-tier sauce variety.

tomatoes.source: Seed Savers Exchange yearbook
tomatoes.notes: West Virginia heirloom, pink-red 1 lb beefsteak. Reliable yields, classic balanced flavor.

tomatoes.source: Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds (Rare Seeds) catalog descriptor
tomatoes.notes: Australian red beefsteak, 1-2 lb ribbed fruit. Heat-tolerant; good for hot southern summers.

tomatoes.source: Seed Savers Exchange yearbook
tomatoes.notes: German heirloom potato-leaf, 1-2 lb pink beefsteak. Considered one of the best-flavored pink tomatoes.

tomatoes.source: Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds (Rare Seeds) catalog descriptor
tomatoes.notes: Italian elongated paste, 4-5 inch finger-shape, very few seeds. Excellent for sun-drying and sauce.

tomatoes.source: Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds (Rare Seeds) catalog descriptor
tomatoes.notes: Larger 12 oz cousin of San Marzano paste, from Mt. Redorta region. Meaty, low juice, sauce powerhouse.

tomatoes.source: Seed Savers Exchange yearbook; Fedco Seeds catalog descriptor
tomatoes.notes: Very early deep-red 4 oz fruit. Concentrated tomato flavor; one of the earliest full-flavored tomatoes for short seasons.

tomatoes.source: Burpee catalog history; Johnny's Selected Seeds variety guide
tomatoes.notes: 1949 Burpee hybrid, 1 lb red beefsteak. Classic American slicer; still a reliable home-garden choice.

tomatoes.source: Burpee catalog; Johnny's Selected Seeds variety guide
tomatoes.notes: Burpee hybrid, 4 oz red fruit, named for July ripening in mid-zones. Useful for early-summer market.

tomatoes.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds variety guide
tomatoes.notes: Determinate Roma-type bred for high lycopene, 4 oz oblong red. Good for sauce and processing.

tomatoes.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds variety guide
tomatoes.notes: Hybrid red cherry, crack-resistant improvement on Sweet 100. Long trusses, very productive.

tomatoes.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds variety guide
tomatoes.notes: Indeterminate red cherry, very high sugar. Disease-resistant; great for kids and snacking.

tomatoes.source: Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds (Rare Seeds) catalog descriptor
tomatoes.notes: Ukrainian saladette, 3 oz dark mahogany with green stripes. Rich, sweet, productive. Showy mixed-color salad combo.

tomatoes.source: Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds (Rare Seeds) catalog descriptor
tomatoes.notes: Green-when-ripe Vernissage line, 3 oz, tropical-tangy. Pick when softening; color stays green with yellow blush.

tomatoes.source: Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds (Rare Seeds) catalog descriptor
tomatoes.notes: Yellow-and-green striped saladette, 3 oz, sweet mild flavor. Best of the Vernissage line for color contrast plates.

tomatoes.source: Seed Savers Exchange yearbook; Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds descriptor
tomatoes.notes: Russian paste, banana-shaped orange 4-5 oz fruit. Sweet flavor; rare orange paste. Sun-dries to amber color.

tomatoes.source: Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds (Rare Seeds) catalog descriptor
tomatoes.notes: Pink elongated paste, 6 inch fruit. Good for slicing and drying. Slow but heavy producer.

tomatoes.source: Seed Savers Exchange yearbook
tomatoes.notes: Italian pear-shaped red cherry, 1 oz. Long productive trusses; pairs visually with Yellow Pear.

tomatoes.source: Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds (Rare Seeds) catalog descriptor
tomatoes.notes: Russian pear-shaped mahogany fruit, 6-8 oz. Rich smoky flavor, low juice; sauce or slicing.

tomatoes.source: Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds (Rare Seeds) catalog descriptor
tomatoes.notes: Yellow pear-shaped 1 oz cherry, prolific. Sweet mild flavor; great for color in mixed cherry mixes.

tomatoes.source: Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds (Rare Seeds) catalog descriptor
tomatoes.notes: Bumpy clustered fruit, the 'traveler tomato': pull off individual lobes as snacks. Sharply acidic, unique novelty.

tomatoes.source: Seed Savers Exchange yearbook
tomatoes.notes: Tiny half-inch red currant tomato, intense full-tomato flavor. Self-sows readily; can become a volunteer weed.

tomatoes.source: Seed Savers Exchange yearbook
tomatoes.notes: Iowa fuzzy pale-yellow 2 oz fruit. Won SSE taste tests multiple times. Stores 2-3 weeks at room temp.

tomatoes.source: Seed Savers Exchange yearbook; Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds descriptor
tomatoes.notes: Pink potato-leaf beefsteak, 1 lb. Considered a flavor rival to Brandywine; named by Ben Quisenberry.

tomatoes.source: Seed Savers Exchange yearbook
tomatoes.notes: Amish-maintained Brandywine line from the Yoder family. 1 lb pink potato-leaf fruit, classic rich flavor.

tomatoes.source: Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds (Rare Seeds) catalog descriptor
tomatoes.notes: Fuzzy yellow with red speckles, 2 oz fruit. Stores well at room temperature like other peach types.

tomatoes.source: Seed Savers Exchange yearbook
tomatoes.notes: Amish-maintained yellow beefsteak, 1 lb, mild and meaty. Heavy-yielding companion to Yoder's Brandywine.

tomatoes.source: Seed Savers Exchange yearbook
tomatoes.notes: Russian dark-mahogany beefsteak, 10-12 oz. Productive and earlier than Black Krim; rich smoky flavor.

tomatoes.source: Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds (Rare Seeds) catalog descriptor
tomatoes.notes: Dark mahogany with green stripes, 2 oz cherry. Sweet smoky flavor; striking salad cherry.

tomatoes.source: Seed Savers Exchange yearbook
tomatoes.notes: Green-when-ripe beefsteak, 1 lb. Sweet tropical flavor; pick when fruit yields to gentle pressure.

tomatoes.source: Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds (Rare Seeds) catalog descriptor
tomatoes.notes: Cream-white beefsteak, 1 lb+. Very low acid, fruity sweet flavor. Largest of the white tomatoes.

tomatoes.source: Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds (Rare Seeds) catalog descriptor
tomatoes.notes: Pale-yellow cherry, very sweet tropical-fruity flavor. Productive trusses; crack-resistant.

tomatoes.source: Seed Savers Exchange yearbook
tomatoes.notes: German cherry, golden-yellow 1 inch fruit in large clusters. Sweet, crack-resistant; very productive.

tomatoes.source: Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds (Rare Seeds) catalog descriptor
tomatoes.notes: Red and gold marbled cherry, often with cat-face star pattern on blossom end. Sweet rich flavor.

tomatoes.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds variety guide
tomatoes.notes: Mahogany-brown 1 inch cherry, rich complex flavor. Long productive trusses; crack-prone if watering swings.

tomatoes.source: Wild Boar Farms catalog; Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds descriptor
tomatoes.notes: Wild Boar Farms purple cherry with green stripes. Sweet-tart, productive, crack-resistant.

tomatoes.source: Wild Boar Farms catalog; Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds descriptor
tomatoes.notes: Green-when-ripe variant of Berkeley Tie-Dye, dark green with yellow stripes. Sweet, fruity flavor.

tomatoes.source: Seed Savers Exchange yearbook
tomatoes.notes: Golden-orange 1 lb beefsteak, sweet rich flavor. Good for southeast humid summers.

tomatoes.source: Seed Savers Exchange yearbook
tomatoes.notes: Mennonite yellow-and-red bicolor beefsteak, 1-2 lb. Sweet rich flavor; show-quality slicer.

tomatoes.source: Seed Savers Exchange yearbook
tomatoes.notes: Pink oxheart, 12 oz, meaty and almost seedless. Great fresh or for sauce.

tomatoes.source: Seed Savers Exchange yearbook
tomatoes.notes: Balkan pink oxheart, 1 lb, meaty texture. Excellent sandwich tomato.

tomatoes.source: Seed Savers Exchange yearbook
tomatoes.notes: Russian pink beefsteak, 12 oz. Rich balanced flavor; cool-tolerant.

tomatoes.source: Fedco Seeds catalog descriptor; Seed Savers Exchange yearbook
tomatoes.notes: Swiss pink slicer, 6-8 oz, thin skin. Sweet floral flavor; harvest at firm ripe.

tomatoes.source: Seed Savers Exchange yearbook
tomatoes.notes: 1898 Salzer Seed introduction, pink beefsteak 1 lb. Classic late-19th-century slicer.

tomatoes.source: Fedco Seeds catalog descriptor; Seed Savers Exchange yearbook
tomatoes.notes: Maine heirloom orange globe, 8-10 oz, low acid. Sweet bright flavor; reliable in cool summers.

tomatoes.source: Seed Savers Exchange yearbook
tomatoes.notes: Yellow variant strain of Kellogg's Breakfast, 1 lb beefsteak, juicy and sweet. Tennessee heirloom.

tomatoes.source: University of Arkansas Cooperative Extension
tomatoes.notes: University of Arkansas release, pink slicer 8 oz, disease-resistant. Reliable in humid south.

tomatoes.source: Seed Savers Exchange yearbook
tomatoes.notes: Pink-yellow bicolor beefsteak, 1 lb, fruity sweet. Reliable Ozark-region producer.

cucumber.notes: Smaller, blockier than slicing types. Pick at 2-4 inches for gherkins, 4-6 for spears. Boston Pickling, National Pickling, Calypso.
Round yellow heirloom; mild, never bitter. Pick at lemon size before seeds harden.
Botanically a melon; ribbed pale-green fruit reaches 24+ inches. Burpless, no bitterness.
Long, slender, thin-skinned (Suyo Long, Tasty Jade). Trellising gives best fruit shape.
Cornell-bred slicing cucumber; reliable, disease-resistant, the workhorse open-pollinated slicer. Marketmore 76 is the modern selection.
1880s heirloom pickling cucumber; vigorous productive vine. Pick at 2-4 inches for cornichons, 4-6 for spears.
Clemson-bred pickling cucumber; bred for downy mildew and bacterial wilt resistance in southeastern climates.
Mexican heirloom climber with grape-sized fruit looking like miniature watermelons; cucumber-lime flavor. Trellis required.
1894 Australian heirloom; round yellow fruit, mild non-bitter. Distinct from existing lemon-cucumber entry by selection lineage; more crack-resistant.
Long pale-green ribbed melon used as cucumber; the snake-like form distinct from existing armenian-cucumber entry. Striped Armenian is the variant.

broccoli.notes: Aspabroc hybrid; tender stem and small florets. Cut-and-come-again for weeks.
1890 Italian heirloom; small main heads (3-4 in) followed by 8-12 weeks of side shoots. Best heirloom for continuous harvest.
1950s UMass selection; cold-hardy fall-cropping broccoli. 4-8 inch heads, sweet after frost.

Tender yellow florets from bolting broccoli plants; mild brassica-honey flavor; salad garnish and stir-fry ingredient; harvest in early morning when fully open. Pollinator favorite.

kaiLan.notes: Chinese broccoli; eat thick stems, leaves, and pre-bolt buds. More heat-tolerant than European broccoli.

kale.notes: Brussels sprouts x kale cross; loose frilly mini-rosettes on a tall stem. Sweetened by frost.
Italian Tuscan heirloom; dark blue-green strap leaves, sweetest after frost. Used for ribollita and kale chips.
1885 Russia/Canada heirloom; oak-shaped leaves with purple veins. More tender than curly kale - good raw in salads.
Most cold-hardy kale (-30F under snow); blue-green flat leaves. Russian/Siberian frontier heirloom.
Vates strain; compact 12-18 inch plants. The classic blue-green curly kale, sweet after frost. Cold-hardy to -10F.
Perennial variegated kale; cream-edged blue-green leaves. 5+ year lifespan if cut back annually. Propagate by cuttings.
Pre-1860 southern heirloom; large cabbage-like 30-36 inch plants. Heat-tolerant for southern gardens; sweetest after frost.
Burgundy-purple curly kale F1 hybrid; extra sweet after frost (color deepens). Ornamental enough for flower beds.

kale.notes: Purple-veined perennial collard. Cutting-propagated. Pick lower leaves continuously; can live 5-8 years.
Tall stem to 6+ feet; harvest leaves over years. Cutting-propagated (rarely flowers). Walking stick cabbage tradition.
