aubergine.notes: Italian rose-pink and white teardrop eggplant, 6 inch fruit. Mild creamy flesh; classic Sicilian variety.

aubergine.source: Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds (Rare Seeds) catalog descriptor
aubergine.notes: Spanish striped purple-and-white eggplant, 6 inch fruit. Mild thin skin; striking color.

aubergine.source: Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds (Rare Seeds) catalog descriptor
aubergine.notes: Pure-white 6 inch oval eggplant. Tender, mild, mushroom-like flavor. Spineless plant.

aubergine.source: Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds (Rare Seeds) catalog descriptor
aubergine.notes: Taiwanese long thin purple eggplant, 12 inch fruit. Tender skin, no need to peel; productive.

aubergine.source: All-America Selections; Johnny's Selected Seeds variety guide
aubergine.notes: Mini purple-and-white striped eggplant, 4 inch fruit. AAS winner; early for short summers.

aubergine.source: Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds (Rare Seeds) catalog descriptor
aubergine.notes: Light green 10 inch slim eggplant. Mild flavor; classic Thai curry vegetable.

aubergine.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds variety guide
aubergine.notes: Lavender-and-cream striped Italian eggplant, 6 inch fruit. Sweet mild; good for grilling.

aubergine.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds variety guide
aubergine.notes: Mini purple eggplant, 4 inch slender fruit. Productive; great whole for grilling.

aubergine.source: Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds (Rare Seeds) catalog descriptor
aubergine.notes: Tomato-shaped fruit ripens orange-red. Used Turkish-style for relishes; pleasantly bitter.

aubergine.source: All-America Selections; Johnny's Selected Seeds variety guide
aubergine.notes: Mini dark purple eggplant, 3-4 inch fruit in clusters. AAS winner; great for containers.

aubergine.source: All-America Selections; Johnny's Selected Seeds variety guide
aubergine.notes: Mini white eggplant, 3 inch teardrop fruit. AAS winner companion to Hansel.

aubergine.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds variety guide
aubergine.notes: Japanese slim purple 10 inch fruit. Productive early hybrid; tender skin.

aubergine.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds variety guide
aubergine.notes: Japanese long deep purple, 10 inch fruit. Hybrid; sets early in cool summers.

aubergine.source: Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds (Rare Seeds) catalog descriptor
aubergine.notes: Ukrainian deep purple long eggplant, 9 inch fruit in clusters. Productive; cold-tolerant.

aubergine.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds variety guide
aubergine.notes: Classic Italian dark purple oval eggplant, 8 inch fruit. Hybrid with strong disease package.

aubergine.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds variety guide
aubergine.notes: Striped purple-and-white Italian eggplant, 6 inch fruit. Compact plants; early producer.

aubergine.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds variety guide
aubergine.notes: Mini Indian-style purple-and-white striped, 3 inch teardrop. Tender skin; productive.

aubergine.source: Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds (Rare Seeds) catalog descriptor
aubergine.notes: Iraqi giant dark purple eggplant, 1-3 lb fruit. Almost no seeds; perfect for baba ghanoush.

aubergine.source: Seed Savers Exchange yearbook; Johnny's Selected Seeds variety guide
aubergine.notes: Classic American Italian-style oval eggplant, 1-3 lb dark purple fruit. Standard slicer.

aubergine.source: Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds (Rare Seeds) catalog descriptor
aubergine.notes: Slender purple Japanese eggplant for tsukemono pickles. Tender skin; harvest at 5-6 inches.

radish.notes: Chinese heirloom; green skin, pink interior. Sweeter and milder than spring radish.
Elongated red-and-white roots; mild, crisp. Quick succession every 10 days for spring/fall crops.
Mixed-color packet (red, white, purple, pink). Good for kids' gardens and farm stand displays.

rocket.notes: Pungent broccoli-like flower buds in spring; peppery leaves. Drought-tolerant once established. Can spread aggressively.
Cream and purple-veined flowers on bolted arugula; intensely peppery sesame-mustard flavor (often stronger than leaves); striking salad garnish and pizza topping.

sweetPotato.notes: Most common orange-flesh cultivar; reliable producer with strong vines. Cure 7-14 days at 80-85F before storage.
Murasaki/Satsuma-imo: purple skin, pale flesh, drier and sweeter than Beauregard. Good for cooler zones with row cover.
Purple-flesh; needs longest season of common cultivars. Best for zone 8+ unless using black plastic and row cover.

legumes.notes: Italian heirloom; elongated red bulbs, sweet enough to eat raw. Long-day type for northern latitudes.
Small 1 inch white onion; classic pickling and stew onion. Direct-sown thickly, harvested young.
Important caveat: only Pisum sativum garden pea blossoms are edible; ornamental sweet pea (Lathyrus odoratus) is TOXIC. Pisum flowers have mild fresh pea flavor; salad and crudite garnish.

legumes.source: UF/IFAS tropical greens notes; UPR Mayaguez specialty crops
legumes.notes: Jamaican / Trinidadian green; tender amaranth leaves stir-fried or stewed. The defining green of Jamaican Saturday-soup callaloo. Cut-and-come-again.

legumes.source: UF/IFAS Tropical Research; UPR Mayaguez specialty crops
legumes.notes: Spiny-fruited chayote strain; same Sechium edule species as smooth chayote with spines that scrub off. Vigorous vine; the defining cho-cho of Caribbean cooking.
Italian red torpedo-shaped onion from Calabria. Intermediate-day, sweet, classic for salads.
AAS bicolor sh2 supersweet. Tender extra-sweet kernels, isolate from other corn types.
Bicolor sugary-enhanced (se). Yellow and white kernels, classic backyard sweet corn.
Early white sugary-enhanced corn. Tender skinned kernels, good for short seasons.
Italian DOP-protected red onion from Calabria. Sweet enough to eat raw like fruit.
Porcelain hardneck from Armenia. Mild for porcelain, sweet when roasted.

beetroot.notes: Doesn't bleed - good for salads. Touchstone Gold is bolt-resistant. Sweeter than red types.
Italian heirloom with concentric red-and-white rings. Color fades on cooking - eat raw or roast briefly.
Cigar-shaped roots; uniform slices for canning. Heirloom Forono and Cylindra cultivars.
1840 heirloom; deep burgundy foliage as ornamental as the root. Harvest leaves at 35 days for salads, roots at 60.
1911 Boston-bred heirloom; flattened-globe shape, dark red, fast to mature. Tall Top strain has the best greens for braising.
Dutch heirloom white beet; sweetest of all beets, doesn't bleed. Good for picky eaters who associate beets with intense color.

sweetcorn.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Corn for Grinding
sweetcorn.notes: Hard kernels for grinding into cornmeal or polenta. Heirloom: Painted Mountain (cold-hardy), Bloody Butcher.

sweetcorn.source: Penn State Extension - Field Corn Production
sweetcorn.notes: Field corn for masa, cornmeal, animal feed. Indentation in kernel as it dries gives the name. Soft starch easier to grind than flint.

sweetcorn.source: Iowa State Extension - Popcorn Production
sweetcorn.notes: Isolate from sweet corn (300+ ft) to prevent cross-pollination. Dry on stalk until kernels glassy, then cure 1+ months.

sweetcorn.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Heirloom Corn
sweetcorn.notes: 1902 Burpee heirloom; the cultivar that made yellow sweet corn popular (white was previously preferred). Open-pollinated; save seed in 100-ft isolation.

sweetcorn.source: UGA Cooperative Extension - Sweet Corn Cultivars
sweetcorn.notes: Late-season white sweet corn hybrid; the gold standard of southern sweet corn. Sweetness diminishes 24 hours post-pick.

sweetcorn.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Heirloom Corn
sweetcorn.notes: 1848 New Jersey heirloom; latest-maturing white sweet corn, holds milk stage 7-10 days. Tall stalks (8-9 ft). Open-pollinated.

sweetcorn.source: Native Seeds/SEARCH - Heritage Corn
sweetcorn.notes: Carl Barnes (Cherokee) flint-corn selection; jewel-toned translucent kernels. ORNAMENTAL CULTIVAR, kernels are decorative not edible fresh. Use Painted Mountain or other flint corn for grinding.

sweetcorn.source: Native Seeds/SEARCH - Heritage Corn
sweetcorn.notes: Mexican Zapotec heirloom flint corn; emerald-green kernels grind into jade-green flour. Used for tamales and atole.

sweetcorn.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Heirloom Corn
sweetcorn.notes: 1890s Connecticut heirloom; 'shoepeg' kernels arranged irregularly with no rows. White, sweet, classic for canning.

sweetcorn.source: Native Seeds/SEARCH - Heritage Corn
sweetcorn.notes: Dave Christensen Montana selection; multicolored flint corn for short-season high-altitude grinding. Edible (unlike Glass Gem).

sweetcorn.source: Native Seeds/SEARCH - Heritage Corn
sweetcorn.notes: Hopi flour corn; deep blue kernels grind into purple-blue cornmeal. Drought-tolerant; traditional southwestern staple.

sweetcorn.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Heirloom Corn
sweetcorn.notes: 1845 Virginia heirloom flint corn; deep red kernels. Good for grinding into red cornmeal and bourbon mashes.

sweetcorn.source: USDA NRCS - Indigenous Heritage Crops
sweetcorn.notes: North Dakota Mandan flint corn; multicolored short-season grinding corn. Drought- and cold-tolerant.

sweetcorn.source: USDA-ARS GRIN-Global accession data; Clemson Cooperative Extension HGIC 1306 sweet corn cultivars.
sweetcorn.notes: Classic late-maturing white (su) sweet corn introduced 1955. Large 8 to 9 inch ears, 14 to 16 rows of tender kernels. Heat tolerant Southern favorite, strong stalks, good husk coverage.

sweetcorn.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension Vegetable Varieties for Gardeners; Johnny's Selected Seeds cultivar descriptions.
sweetcorn.notes: Bicolor sugary (su) hybrid with yellow and white kernels in equal mix. 7 to 8 inch ears, balanced sweetness, fresh-eating standard for mid-season harvest.

sweetcorn.source: Penn State Extension Vegetable Variety Guide; Crookham Company breeder notes.
sweetcorn.notes: Yellow sugary-enhanced (se) hybrid known for reliable performance and rich corn flavor. 8 inch ears with 16 to 18 rows; tolerant of cool spring soils relative to other se types.

sweetcorn.source: University of Massachusetts Extension Vegetable Program; Harris Seeds variety database.
sweetcorn.notes: Bicolor sugary-enhanced (se) hybrid, 8 inch ears, very tender pericarp. Widely planted for farm stand and home garden mid-season slot.

sweetcorn.source: Ohio State University Extension Bulletin 672 Sweet Corn Production; Seminis variety profile.
sweetcorn.notes: Yellow sugary-enhanced (se) hybrid with large 9 inch ears and excellent tip fill. Strong stalks resist lodging; popular for late summer fresh market.

sweetcorn.source: University of Minnesota Extension Yard and Garden News; Johnny's Selected Seeds cultivar database.
sweetcorn.notes: Bicolor sugary-enhanced (se) early hybrid bred for short seasons. 7 inch ears, productive on 5 to 6 foot stalks. Often the first sweet corn ready in Northern markets.

sweetcorn.source: University of Illinois Extension Hort Answers; Mirai brand cultivar literature (Sakata Seed America).
sweetcorn.notes: Japanese-bred synergistic (sy) series combining su, se, and sh2 kernels for exceptional tenderness and sweetness. Available in bicolor and yellow strains; premium fresh-market type.

sweetcorn.source: Wisconsin Vegetable Crop Updates UW-Madison Extension; Rogers (Syngenta) variety archive.
sweetcorn.notes: Yellow sugary (su) processing and fresh-market standard. 8 to 9 inch ears with 16 to 20 rows of golden kernels; widely planted across canning belt since 1960s.

sweetcorn.source: University of Illinois Department of Crop Sciences sweet corn breeding history; Ag Alumni Seed variety notes.
sweetcorn.notes: University of Illinois supersweet (sh2) yellow corn, released 1970s. High sugar, slow conversion to starch giving long shelf life. Requires warm soils; do not plant near su or se types.

sweetcorn.source: Seed Savers Exchange heirloom collection notes; USDA-ARS GRIN PI accession records.
sweetcorn.notes: Heirloom white sugary (su) introduced 1848 by Nathan Stowell of New Jersey. 8 to 9 inch ears, holds milk stage unusually long, hence the Evergreen name. 8 foot stalks.

sweetcorn.source: Seed Savers Exchange Garden Seed Inventory; W. Atlee Burpee Company catalog archives.
sweetcorn.notes: Heirloom yellow sugary (su) introduced 1902 by Burpee. 6 to 7 inch ears on 5 to 6 foot stalks; the variety that made yellow corn popular in America. Open-pollinated, seed-saving friendly.

sweetcorn.source: All-America Selections 2001 vegetable winners; Crookham Company variety descriptions.
sweetcorn.notes: Yellow synergistic (sy) hybrid, 2001 All-America Selections winner. 8 to 9 inch ears with 16 to 18 rows; balanced sweetness and creamy texture, vigorous emergence.

sweetcorn.source: Seeds Trust catalog and Dave Christensen breeding documentation; Organic Seed Alliance grower trials.
sweetcorn.notes: Open-pollinated short-season flint developed by Dave Christensen in Montana over 30 years, combining 70+ Native American and homesteader strains. 5 to 6 foot stalks, multicolored kernels for flour and meal.

sweetcorn.source: Native Seeds/SEARCH variety notes; Greg Schoen and Carl Barnes breeding records.
sweetcorn.notes: Translucent jewel-toned flint developed by Carl Barnes (Cherokee) from Pawnee and Osage parent lines. Ornamental and grindable; 3 to 4 inch ears with iridescent kernels in every color.

sweetcorn.source: Seed Savers Exchange and USDA-ARS GRIN North Dakota landrace records.
sweetcorn.notes: Heirloom flint from the Mandan people of the upper Missouri River. Multicolored 7 inch ears on 5 foot stalks; cold tolerant and drought tolerant, prized for cornmeal and parching.

sweetcorn.source: Vermont Land Trust seed library; High Mowing Organic Seeds variety notes.
sweetcorn.notes: Vermont heirloom flint from Roy Fair of Calais, descended from Abenaki seed. 8 inch ears with maroon and yellow kernels; excellent for jonnycake meal and polenta in cold climates.

sweetcorn.source: Native Seeds/SEARCH Hopi traditional varieties collection; USDA-ARS GRIN southwestern landrace records.
sweetcorn.notes: Ancient Hopi flour corn from the American Southwest. Deep blue kernels on 8 to 10 inch ears; soft starch grinds into traditional piki bread flour. Drought adapted, tolerates poor soils.

sweetcorn.source: Native Seeds/SEARCH variety database; New Mexico State University Extension publication H-232.
sweetcorn.notes: Rio Grande Pueblo blue flour corn, slightly larger and later than Hopi Blue. Used for atole, chaquegue, and blue cornbread. Adapted to high desert and intermountain conditions.

sweetcorn.source: Seed Savers Exchange; North Dakota State University historical landrace inventory.
sweetcorn.notes: Northern Plains flour corn from the Mandan people, rose-pink kernels on 6 to 7 inch ears. Short season and cold tolerant; soft starch ideal for traditional cornmeal preparations.

sweetcorn.source: Cornell Vegetable Varieties Mache
sweetcorn.notes: French winter salad green; small tender nutty rosettes; harvests possible at 30F under low tunnels. Standard fall and winter rotation crop in market gardens.

sweetcorn.source: Native Seeds/SEARCH Carl Barnes archive; Seed Savers Exchange
sweetcorn.notes: Cherokee multicolored flint corn selected by Carl Barnes from his Pawnee, Osage, and Cherokee heritage lines. Translucent jewel-toned kernels; flour or popcorn use.

sweetcorn.source: Seed Savers Exchange Anishinaabe heritage seed; Dream of Wild Health
sweetcorn.notes: Northern Minnesota Anishinaabe heritage flint corn; 90-day short-season ultra-cold-tolerant grain corn. Multicolored hard kernels for hominy and parched corn.

sweetcorn.source: Native Seeds/SEARCH; Dream of Wild Health seed sovereignty project
sweetcorn.notes: Mandan / Hidatsa Missouri River heritage soft flour corn; multicolored short ears, traditional bread and flour corn. Repatriated through Native Seeds/SEARCH and Dream of Wild Health.

sweetcorn.source: Seed Savers Exchange yearbook
sweetcorn.notes: 1890s white shoepeg heirloom. Sweet creamy kernels in irregular rows.

sweetcorn.source: Seed Savers Exchange yearbook
sweetcorn.notes: Pre-1864 heirloom, kernels mature to blue-black. Sweet at milk stage, harvest while white.

sweetcorn.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds catalog descriptor
sweetcorn.notes: Yellow sugary-enhanced corn, purple-tinged husks. Sweet flavor, holds well after harvest.

sweetcorn.source: Seed Savers Exchange yearbook
sweetcorn.notes: 1902 New Jersey heirloom white sweet corn. Small short ears with excellent flavor.

sweetcorn.source: Fedco Seeds catalog descriptor
sweetcorn.notes: Open-pollinated yellow sweet corn. Old-fashioned corny flavor, true to type.

sweetcorn.source: Fedco Seeds catalog descriptor
sweetcorn.notes: Open-pollinated bicolor sweet corn from Fedco. Yellow and white kernels, reliable for seed-saving.

sweetcorn.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds catalog descriptor
sweetcorn.notes: Yellow sugary-enhanced sweet corn. Bright flavor, dependable for fresh eating.

sweetcorn.source: Fedco Seeds catalog descriptor
sweetcorn.notes: Open-pollinated white sweet corn from Idaho. Old-fashioned corny flavor, seed-saving variety.

sweetcorn.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds catalog descriptor
sweetcorn.notes: Yellow sh2 supersweet for northern gardens. Isolate from other corn types to maintain sweetness.

sweetcorn.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds catalog descriptor
sweetcorn.notes: Early yellow sugary-enhanced corn. Tolerates cool soils, productive in short seasons.

sweetcorn.source: Baker Creek catalog descriptor
sweetcorn.notes: Purple-husked white-kernel sweet corn. Ornamental and delicious, open-pollinated.

okra.notes: Red pods turn green when cooked. Ornamental in vegetable garden. AAS winner.
Campbell Soup Co. heirloom (1950s); smooth round (vs ribbed) deep-green pods. Tender even at 7-8 inches. Reliable in humid summers.
Compact early hybrid bred for Louisiana double-cropping; spineless dark-green pods; productive even in shorter SE seasons. AAS 2013 winner.
1988 AAS winner from Clemson; deep burgundy red pods and stems; color fades to green when cooked. Pick young at 3 inches for tenderness.

okra.source: Southern Exposure Seed Exchange Cowhorn okra
okra.notes: SE US heirloom; unusual long curved cowhorn-shaped pods that remain tender at 7 to 9 inches; deep ribbing; productive over long summers.

okra.source: Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds Red Velvet okra
okra.notes: Beautiful deep red ribbed pods on red stems; sweeter than green okra varieties; pods remain tender longer than Burgundy. Ornamental in kitchen gardens.

artichoke.notes: Violetta di Chioggia, Purple of Romagna. Distinct from existing globe-artichoke entry (cultivar group). Bud color is ornamental as well as edible.
Annual-flowering selection; reliable first-year buds without vernalization. Standard for short-summer zones.
Classic perennial green-headed cultivar. Heavy yields in 2nd-3rd year. California staple.

artichoke.source: Baker Creek catalog descriptor; Seed Savers Exchange
artichoke.notes: Italian heirloom. Elongated purple buds. Tender hearts for grilling and stuffing.
Israeli annual cultivar. Reliable first-year crops without vernalization. Smaller more uniform buds.
Italian heirloom, purple-tinged conical heads. Tender thornless inner leaves; classic for Roman fried artichokes.
Thornless commercial cultivar; meaty hearts up to 5 in diameter. California-bred.
Cold-hardy perennial selection; survives to zone 4 with mulch. Reliable in upper Midwest.

pumpkin.notes: French heirloom; deeply ribbed crimson Cinderella-style pumpkin. Eating quality good though looks more decorative.
Maine heirloom; cylindrical flat-on-top fruit looks like an overgrown zucchini. Sweet dry flesh, prized for pies; cures from green to orange.
Small (4-7 lb) New England pie pumpkin; sweeter and drier than carving pumpkins. Small Sugar and New England Pie are common cultivars.
Carving pumpkin; 10-25 lb fruit. Connecticut Field is the heritage standard. Stringy bland flesh - decorative use, not pie.
French heirloom moschata pumpkin; flat lobed wheel shape, tan skin ripening to deep brown. Sweet smooth flesh; vine borer resistant (C. moschata trait).

pumpkin.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; UNH Extension pumpkin guide
pumpkin.notes: Carving-pumpkin types: Howden, Connecticut Field, Autumn Gold. 10 to 25 lb round orange Cucurbita pepo, ridged. Hollow for carving, marginal eating quality.

pumpkin.source: UNH Extension; Johnny's Selected Seeds variety notes
pumpkin.notes: Small (4 to 8 lb) sugar pumpkins for cooking. New England Pie, Sugar Pie, Winter Luxury are standard cultivars. Dense flesh, low water content.

pumpkin.source: Seed Savers Exchange; Cornell variety database
pumpkin.notes: Rouge Vif d'Etampes type Cucurbita maxima; flat red-orange ribbed pumpkin, 15 to 25 lb. Excellent eating, dramatic appearance.

pumpkin.source: Seed Savers Exchange; French heritage seed catalogs
pumpkin.notes: Provencal Cucurbita moschata; flat ribbed 20 lb fruit, mottled green ripening to deep tan. Sweet dense flesh, vine borer resistant, the gold-standard French cooking pumpkin.

chard.notes: AAS winner; rainbow-stemmed chard mix (red, pink, orange, yellow, white). Both ornamental and edible.
1934 Burpee selection; thick white stems and dark crinkled leaves. Heat- and frost-tolerant; cut-and-come-again all season.
Heirloom red-stemmed chard; deep burgundy stems and veins. Less heat-tolerant than Fordhook but more ornamental.
Open-pollinated multi-color blend; less uniform than F1 'Bright Lights' but seed-saveable. Five Color Silverbeet is the heirloom strain.

asparagus.notes: Rutgers all-male hybrid; vigorous, rust-resistant, longer productive life than older Jersey series. The current standard for new asparagus beds.
Anthocyanin-rich purple spears (cooks to dark green); 20% sweeter, less stringy. Mixed-sex (M and F), produces some berries unlike Jersey hybrids.

asparagus.source: USDA GRIN germplasm descriptor; Seed Savers Exchange yearbook
asparagus.notes: Classic open-pollinated heirloom. Rust-resistant. Mix male and female; lower yields than all-male hybrids.

asparagus.source: Rutgers Cooperative Extension; Johnny's Selected Seeds
asparagus.notes: Rutgers all-male hybrid. Heavy yields, rust and fusarium resistant. Tolerates cold winters well.
All-male hybrid. Early-emerging; thrives in lighter soils. High yields with strong disease package.
Australian purple-spear cultivar. Sweet, mild, lower fiber. Color fades when cooked; eat raw or briefly cooked.
California-adapted all-male hybrid. Heat tolerant; productive in southern climates. Smooth tight tips.
Heat-tolerant southern California-adapted hybrid. Strong yields in transition zones too warm for Jersey series.

asparagus.source: University of Guelph release; Johnny's Selected Seeds catalog
asparagus.notes: University of Guelph all-male hybrid. Best cold-climate yield/persistence. Replaces older Jersey series in north.

rhubarb.notes: 1837 English heirloom; green stalks with red blush, classic baking rhubarb. Productive and reliable; the most widely-grown cultivar.
Very cold-hardy rhubarb; deep red stalks throughout (no green core). Sweeter than Victoria. Top choice for far-north gardens.

rhubarb.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; OSU rhubarb variety notes
rhubarb.notes: Deep red full-length stalks; sweeter than Victoria, premier color for pie and compote. Vigorous, productive.
Speckled red stalks, slightly less stringy. Holds color well in pies. Reliable in heavy clay.
Comes true from seed. Lower oxalic acid; harvest longer into summer than most cultivars.

rhubarb.source: University of Saskatchewan; Veseys Seeds catalog
rhubarb.notes: Canadian cultivar. Tender bright red stalks; mild flavor. Resists crown rot in wet sites.
Bright red stalks throughout. Sweet, mild flavor; popular for pies. Vigorous and productive.
Extra-large green stalks; vigorous. Late bolt; good for cooler-summer regions.

tomatillo.notes: Purple-skinned tomatillo (vs green). Sweeter than green types when fully ripe; husks split as fruit fills. Distinct from existing tomatillo entry.

tomatillo.source: Native Seeds/SEARCH catalog; Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds descriptor
tomatillo.notes: Classic Mexican green tomatillo, 1-2 inch fruit. Tart bright flavor; foundation of salsa verde. Plant at least two for pollination.

tomatillo.source: Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds (Rare Seeds) catalog descriptor
tomatillo.notes: Giant green tomatillo, 2-3 inch fruit. Productive; mild tart flavor.
Heirloom Mexican green tomatillo, traditional salsa verde variety. Plant 2+ for cross-pollination.

tomatillo.source: Native Seeds/SEARCH catalog; Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds descriptor
tomatillo.notes: Guatemalan purple-skinned tomatillo from Coban. Sweeter than green tomatillo; great fresh.
Wild Mexican milpa tomatillo, small purple-yellow fruit. Intense tart flavor for traditional salsas.

mustardGreens.notes: 1935 southern AAS heirloom; bright-green frilled-edge leaves, mildly hot mustard flavor. The standard southern table-mustard cultivar.
Large smooth oval leaves 12 to 15 inches; mild peppery flavor; quick growing for braised greens and SE mustard pot likker.

mustardGreens.source: Univ of Florida IFAS Mustard greens
mustardGreens.notes: Bright yellow mustard flowers and unopened buds; sharp peppery wasabi-like flavor; garnish for sushi, salads, and braises; can be lightly pickled or fermented.

cauliflower.notes: Italian heirloom; deep purple anthocyanin-rich heads. Color cooks to green when boiled - steam or roast briefly to retain.
Cornell-bred orange cauliflower (1980s); 25x the beta-carotene of white. Color holds when cooked. Tighter heads than purple types.
Modern F1 deep-purple cauliflower; brighter and more uniform than Italian heirloom. Holds color when steamed (vs boiled).

fennel.source: Cornell vegetable variety database; Johnny's Selected Seeds notes
fennel.notes: Swiss bolt-resistant bulbing fennel; tight white bulbs, mild anise flavor. The standard market Florence fennel.

watercress.source: Kitazawa Seed; Rocky Mountain Watercress Co
watercress.notes: Aquatic edge layer. Edible peppery leaves, vitamin C and K dense. Cool flowing water ideal, but moist garden bed also works. Hardy perennial.

watercress.source: Plants for a Future; Adaptive Seeds catalog
watercress.notes: Pungent strain selected for intense horseradish-like heat. Use only in clean cold water; never wild-collected streams.

watercress.source: Royal Horticultural Society; Plants for a Future
watercress.notes: Classic UK strain for chalk-stream cultivation. Milder than wasabi-flavored strain. Cool-water requires 50-60F.

tatsoi.notes: Flat-rosetted Asian green; thick dark green spoon-shaped leaves with mild mustard flavor; harvest whole rosette or pick outer leaves. Very cold tolerant for winter harvests.

tatsoi.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds variety notes; Kitazawa Seed Company
tatsoi.notes: Japanese rosette green; flat spoon-shaped dark green leaves. Cold-hardy to 15 F; the standard salad-mix winter green.

pakChoi.notes: Compact green-stemmed Shanghai-type bok choy; tender spoon-shaped leaves and crisp celadon-green stems; mild sweet flavor; ideal stir-fry size at 6 inches.

pakChoi.source: Johnny Seeds Mei Qing Choi technical sheet
pakChoi.notes: Miniature pak choi reaching only 3 to 4 inches at maturity; ideal for high-density baby head harvests; popular Asian market variety. Productive year-round in cool conditions.

choySum.notes: Chinese flowering brassica; harvest tender flowering shoots with yellow buds; sweet mild flavor; staple of Cantonese stir-fries with oyster sauce.

choySum.source: Kitazawa Seed Company variety notes; UH Manoa Cooperative Extension
choySum.notes: Cantonese flowering brassica harvested at the bud stage; tender stem and small yellow flowers. The defining green for chinese stir-fry oyster sauce dishes.

perennialVegetables.source: Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds (Rare Seeds) catalog descriptor
perennialVegetables.notes: Golden husk fruit, marble-sized. Sweet tropical flavor; pick after fruit drops in husk.
Productive Wisconsin selection, larger fruit than Aunt Molly's. Sweet butterscotch-pineapple flavor.

courgette.notes: Classic black-green bush zucchini, 1957 AAS winner. Prolific, reliable.
Italian heirloom ribbed zucchini with nutty flavor. Holds quality at larger sizes than typical zucchini.
Round dark green zucchini, AAS winner. Compact plants, great for stuffing.
Italian heirloom striped zucchini, 1949 AAS. Bushy, productive, gray-green and dark-green stripes.
Italian striped heirloom zucchini, long slender fruit. Firm-fleshed, holds in the field.
Hybrid green zucchini with mottled stripes. Heavy yielding, virus-resistant.
Organic-bred hybrid dark green zucchini. Open plant habit, virus-resistant.
AAS golden-yellow zucchini. Bright color holds in cooking.

celery.source: Adaptive Seeds catalog descriptor; Plants for a Future
celery.notes: Variegated pink-white-green form. Edible Asian water dropwort (seri); celery-parsley flavor. Note: don't confuse with toxic O. crocata.

newTypes.notes: Italian saltwort; needles look like rosemary, taste like spinach. Tolerates saline soil.
Egyptian spinach; mucilaginous when cooked, slick texture. Loves heat, no bolting issues in summer.
Caribbean leafy amaranth; cut-and-come-again. Tolerates extreme heat.
Trellis required; fruit can curl - hang weights for straight gourds. Use immature like cucumber.
Coastal perennial with leaves tasting like oysters. Difficult to establish from seed; prefers sandy, alkaline soil.
Bumpy green fruit (Indian/Chinese types differ). Eat immature; mature fruit too bitter and orange. Soak/salt to reduce bitterness.
Squash-family climber; plant whole sprouting fruit. Tubers, fruits, shoots all edible. Perennial in zone 8+.
Austrian-Italian heirloom romaine; same speckle pattern as Forellenschluss. Distinct cultivar group of cos types with mottled leaves.
PNW/Rocky Mountain spring ephemeral; tiny edible corms (potato-like). Indigenous food source; harvest sustainably (replant smallest).

newTypes.source: Eric Toensmeier Perennial Vegetables; UC Davis Cucurbit
newTypes.notes: Vine layer cucurbit perennial in warm zones. Edible fruit (mild summer squash), shoots, tubers, leaves. Single plant 30 to 100 fruit. Mexican staple.

newTypes.source: Salba Corp variety registration; Coates and Ayerza, Industrial Crops and Products 11 (2009)
newTypes.notes: Salba is a white-seeded chia (Salvia hispanica) variety trademarked by Salba Corp, selected for higher oil and protein than wild chia. Short-day flowering means North American grain production limited to southern zones.
Edible when young (6 inches); leave on vine 4+ months for mature bath/scrub sponge. SE US commercial crop in zones 8-10.
Smooth luffa harvested young as si gua in Chinese cooking; mild zucchini-like flavor; popular in stir-fries and soups across SE Asia.
Italian winter salad; thin antler-shaped leaves with slightly salty mineral flavor; star ingredient in mesclun mixes. Very cold tolerant for unheated winter production.
Italian seasonal salad green; thin grassy succulent shoots with bright saline mineral flavor; brief 4 to 6 week window in spring. Tolerates saline soils where most crops fail.
African ornamental edible; deep maroon-burgundy maple-shaped leaves with tart cranberry-like flavor; eaten cooked or in salads; brews into deep red tea. Hardy perennial in zone 9+.

newTypes.source: Kitazawa Seed Company variety notes; UF/IFAS tropical cucurbits
newTypes.notes: Asian ridged luffa; eat at 6 to 8 inches like zucchini. Past 12 inches fiber develops and they become sponge gourds. The defining Vietnamese muop khia.

newTypes.source: Kitazawa Seed Company variety notes; UH Manoa Cooperative Extension
newTypes.notes: Pale-green smooth-skinned bitter gourd; milder than the warty Indian type. The defining vegetable of stir-fried bitter melon and Okinawan champuru.

newTypes.source: Kitazawa Seed Company variety notes; UH Manoa Cooperative Extension
newTypes.notes: Large pale waxy melon, 20 to 50 lb; mild cucumber-like flesh used in soup, stir-fry, traditional Chinese banquet sculptures. Stores 6+ months at room temperature.

newTypes.source: Kitazawa Seed Company variety notes; UH Manoa Cooperative Extension
newTypes.notes: Small fuzzy-skinned summer-harvested winter melon relative; tender flesh used in soup and stir-fry. The young harvest of the winter melon family.

newTypes.source: UF/IFAS tropical cucurbits notes; Indian Agricultural Research Institute records
newTypes.notes: South Asian climbing cucurbit; 3 to 6 ft long pale green snake-like fruits, cucumber-like flesh. The defining vegetable of South Indian pudalangai kootu.

newTypes.source: FAO traditional African crops; USDA-ARS plant introduction collection
newTypes.notes: West African seed-melon; bitter inedible flesh, but the seeds are ground to thicken egusi soup. Drought tolerant relative of watermelon.
Ethiopian tef cultivar with cream-colored seed; the defining grain of injera flatbread. Drought tolerant; gluten-free traditional grain.

newTypes.source: Plants for a Future; Edible Forest Gardens
newTypes.notes: Japanese perennial. Blanched young shoots eaten raw or cooked. Cucumber-like crisp asparagus alternative.

newTypes.source: Plants for a Future; Edible Forest Gardens
newTypes.notes: Japanese angelica tree. Tender unfurling spring shoots prized tempura ingredient. Limit harvest to avoid weakening.

newTypes.source: ECHO Community; University of Florida IFAS
newTypes.notes: Corm-producing aquatic sedge. Paddy-grown like rice. Sweet crisp corms; harvest after foliage dies back in fall.

newTypes.source: ECHO Community; Plants for a Future
newTypes.notes: Southeast Asian leaf vegetable. Young leaves and flower buds cooked. Aggressively invasive; site in contained ponds.
Aquatic dock species. Young leaves edible cooked (oxalic acid; rotate). Mineral accumulator for wet permaculture sites.

newTypes.source: Native Seeds/SEARCH seed bank descriptor; Seed Savers Exchange yearbook
newTypes.notes: Hopi heritage cushaw, striped tan and green fruit. Dry-farmed in arid Southwest by Pueblo peoples for centuries. Drought-resilient long vines.
